agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (stock) with Sales/Order Management (orders) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This crosses two established bounded contexts and introduces tight coupling where loose coupling previously existed by design.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request implies merging concerns that were deliberately separated — this is the core domain risk.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer >= 0 representing the current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management)."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase one or more products. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A single line item within an Order, referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Product","definition":"An item that exists in the warehouse and can be ordered. The identity is shared across contexts, but each context owns different attributes."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A domain concept currently MISSING from both contexts. Represents a temporary claim on stock that has been committed to an order but not yet picked. Essential for correct integration."},{"term":"Blocked Order","definition":"Proposed new state for an Order that cannot be fulfilled due to insufficient stock. Needs precise definition — is it rejected, held for review, or queued?"},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"A read-model projection of StockLevel from Context A surfaced into Context B's UI. Must be understood as eventually consistent, not real-time truth."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for any Product must never go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Negative stock represents a physically impossible state — you cannot ship items that do not exist. If violated, the warehouse data is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"For every Product, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, across all concurrent operations."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order must not be confirmed if the sum of requested Quantity across all OrderLines exceeds the available (unreserved) StockLevel for any referenced Product.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy decision. Some businesses deliberately allow overselling (backorders, pre-orders, drop-shipping). A VP could change this policy. It is not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X with available stock 5 and no reservations, when an Order requests Quantity 6 of Product X, then the Order is blocked."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock availability shown in the Sales context is eventually consistent with the Warehouse context — it is NOT a guarantee of real-time truth.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a distributed systems constraint, not a policy choice. Two separate bounded contexts cannot share real-time state without merging into one. Treating it as real-time creates false guarantees and race conditions.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator in Context B may lag behind the true StockLevel in Context A by a bounded time window (e.g., seconds). The UI must never label this as 'guaranteed available.'"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A Stock Reservation must be created when an Order is accepted, and released if the Order is cancelled or expires. Reservations reduce available stock without reducing physical StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without reservations, two concurrent orders can both see stock=5 and both order 5 units, resulting in overselling or BR-1 violation during picking. The reservation is required for correctness under concurrency.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X with StockLevel 10 and existing reservations totaling 7, available stock = 3. A new order for Quantity 4 must be blocked."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The 'blocked' decision must be made by the context that owns stock truth (Warehouse Management), not by the Sales context reading a stale projection.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If Sales makes the blocking decision based on its local read model, a race condition exists: the projection may be stale. The authoritative check must occur in or be delegated to the stock-owning context to prevent corruption of BR-1.","testable_assertion":"The order acceptance flow must include a synchronous or transactionally consistent stock check against Context A's authoritative data, not against Context B's local cache."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Product with StockLevel 10 and 0 existing reservations","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity 10 of that Product","then":"The Order is accepted and a Stock Reservation of 10 is created, reducing available stock to 0."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Product with StockLevel 10 and existing reservations of 8","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for Quantity 3 of that Product","then":"The Order is blocked because available stock (2) is less than requested Quantity (3). No reservation is created."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Two customers simultaneously place Orders each requesting Quantity 5 of a Product with StockLevel 5 and 0 reservations","when":"Both Orders are processed concurrently","then":"Exactly one Order is accepted and one is blocked. StockLevel never goes negative. No double-reservation occurs."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An accepted Order with a Stock Reservation of 5","when":"The Order is cancelled by the customer","then":"The Stock Reservation is released and available stock increases by 5."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Product's StockLevel changes in Context A","when":"The sales team views the product in Context B","then":"The stock available indicator reflects the change within a bounded time window, and is clearly labeled as an estimate, not a guarantee."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Product with StockLevel 0","when":"A customer attempts to place an Order for that Product","then":"The Order is blocked immediately. The customer receives a clear message that the item is out of stock."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An Order with multiple OrderLines","when":"Stock is insufficient for ANY single OrderLine","then":"The entire Order is blocked, not partially fulfilled (unless partial fulfillment is an explicit business policy — see escalation)."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Race condition: stock decremented by warehouse receiving a pick confirmation at the exact moment an order is being validated","expected_behavior":"The reservation system must use optimistic or pessimistic concurrency control. One operation wins; the other retries or fails gracefully."},{"scenario":"Reservation expiry: customer places order, reservation created, but customer never completes payment","expected_behavior":"Reservations must have a TTL. Expired reservations are released back to available stock. The TTL duration is a business policy decision."},{"scenario":"Bulk order: a single order requests more than total warehouse capacity for a product","expected_behavior":"Order is blocked. The system should not create a partial reservation."},{"scenario":"Stock adjustment (inventory correction, damage, loss) reduces StockLevel below existing reservations","expected_behavior":"Existing reservations must be honored (they represent commitments). The system must alert operations that stock is over-committed and some orders may not be fulfillable."},{"scenario":"Split shipment: can an order be partially fulfilled if some items are in stock and others are not?","expected_behavior":"This is undefined in the current spec and must be a business decision. See escalation."},{"scenario":"Supplier delivery increases stock while orders are in 'blocked' queue","expected_behavior":"The system should have a mechanism to re-evaluate blocked orders when stock is replenished, or notify the sales team. Without this, blocked orders are silently lost."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders prevents overselling, which reduces costly backorder fulfillment, customer compensation, and reputational damage. However, false blocks due to stale data or overly aggressive reservation logic can cause lost revenue. The reservation TTL is a direct lever on revenue vs. risk tradeoff.","operational":"Introduces significant operational complexity: a new Stock Reservation concept must be managed, reservation expiry must be monitored, and the integration between two contexts adds a failure mode. Warehouse operations must now account for reserved vs. physical stock in their planning.","user":"Customers get faster feedback on availability (positive). However, if the indicator says 'available' but the order is then blocked due to eventual consistency lag, trust erodes. The UX must set correct expectations — 'estimated availability' not 'in stock.'","risk":"HIGH. Cross-context integration is the single most common source of domain corruption. If implemented as a direct database query from Sales into Warehouse, it violates bounded context boundaries and creates a distributed monolith. If implemented via events without reservations, race conditions will cause overselling. The implementation pattern chosen here has outsized architectural consequences.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain visibility into stock but may experience frustration if availability indicator is inaccurate. Blocked orders need clear communication and potentially a waitlist or notification option."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains real-time(ish) stock visibility, reducing time spent on manual stock checks. Must understand the indicator is an estimate. Workflow changes needed for handling blocked orders."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Must now account for reservations in their picking and replenishment workflows. Physical stock vs. available stock becomes a critical distinction in daily operations."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must implement a cross-context integration pattern (anti-corruption layer, domain events, or shared kernel). The choice has long-term architectural implications."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"Blocked orders that are later released need clear audit trails. Reservation expiry must be tracked to prevent phantom commitments on the books."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec has fundamental ambiguities: (1) 'automatically block' is undefined — reject? hold? queue? (2) 'real time' is physically impossible across bounded contexts and the acceptable lag is unspecified, (3) no mention of the Stock Reservation concept which is essential for correctness, (4) no policy on partial fulfillment, (5) no definition of what happens to blocked orders (dead end or recoverable?). These are not implementation details — they are core business decisions that change the domain model.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 is a true invariant","A reservation or equivalent mechanism is required for concurrent correctness","The stock indicator in Sales cannot be real-time truth — it is eventually consistent","The authoritative stock check must be owned by Context A"],"low_confidence_areas":["What 'blocked' means as an Order state (rejected vs. held vs. queued)","Whether partial fulfillment is allowed","Reservation TTL policy","What happens to blocked orders when stock is replenished","Whether overselling should ever be permitted (backorders, pre-orders)"],"assumptions":["The two bounded contexts are deployed as separate services or modules with independent data stores","There is no existing Stock Reservation concept in either context","'Block' means prevent the order from progressing, not cancel it retroactively","The business wants strict stock enforcement, not soft warnings"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The term 'block' is undefined. Does blocking an order mean: (A) rejecting it outright with an error, (B) holding it in a pending state for manual review by sales, or (C) queuing it for automatic fulfillment when stock arrives? Each option has radically different domain models, UX, and operational implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Reject: Order is refused, customer must retry later","(B) Hold: Order enters a 'Pending Stock' state, sales team manually releases or cancels","(C) Queue: Order is accepted but flagged, automatically released when stock arrives"],"recommendation":"Option B — Hold for review. It preserves the customer relationship, gives the sales team control, and avoids silent order loss. But this is a business decision, not a technical one."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' stock indicator is physically impossible across bounded contexts. What is the acceptable staleness window? Seconds? Minutes? This determines the integration pattern (synchronous query vs. event-driven projection vs. polling).","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["Sub-second via synchronous API call to Context A (tight coupling)","Seconds via domain events and CQRS read model (recommended)","Minutes via periodic batch sync (simplest but least useful)"],"recommendation":"Event-driven projection with sub-5-second staleness, clearly labeled as 'estimated availability' in the UI."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No Stock Reservation concept exists in either context. This is a prerequisite for correct implementation. Without it, concurrent orders will race on the same stock, violating BR-1 or causing overselling. Who owns the Reservation — Warehouse or Sales? This is a strategic bounded context ownership decision.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Warehouse Team","Sales Team"],"options":["Reservation owned by Warehouse context (closer to stock truth, simpler consistency)","Reservation owned by a new shared context (Fulfillment/Allocation)","Reservation owned by Sales context (dangerous — makes stock decisions in the wrong context)"],"recommendation":"Reservation owned by Warehouse context, exposed via an explicit ReserveStock command. Sales context sends a reservation request; Warehouse confirms or denies."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partial fulfillment policy is undefined. If an Order has 3 OrderLines and only 2 have sufficient stock, should the order be (A) entirely blocked, (B) partially accepted with the available items, or (C) split into two orders?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team","Finance/Accounting"],"options":["(A) All-or-nothing: entire order blocked if any line insufficient","(B) Partial accept: ship what's available, block the rest","(C) Split: create sub-orders per availability"],"recommendation":"Default to all-or-nothing (A) for simplicity and customer clarity, with partial fulfillment as a future enhancement. But this must be confirmed by the business."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The feature request implies Sales context should directly know and enforce stock levels, which violates bounded context separation.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5 — Stock truth must be owned by Warehouse Management. Sales must request stock validation, not perform it locally."},{"violation":"The term 'real time' creates a false expectation of synchronous cross-context state sharing, which is architecturally dishonest and will lead to bugs that erode user trust.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3 — Stock availability across contexts is eventually consistent, not real-time."}]}

This crosses two established bounded contexts and introduces tight coupling where loose coupling previously existed by design.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
